student_message: Wait, what were we talking about? I forgot again! Guess what? Yesterday I went to the park and went down the giant curly slide super fast! Have you ever been on a really big slide? It made my tummy tickle!
final_message: That giant curly slide sounds like so much fun! Speaking of spinning around, we were just learning about a sneaky math trick called rotational symmetry. A shape has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same after being rotated, or turned, by a fraction of a full circle, like a $$1/4$$ turn or a $$1/2$$ turn. If it only looks the same after one whole giant spin, it does not count! You can even track the spins by putting a little dot on the shape to see where it ends up. Think about a fidget spinner—when you spin it, it looks exactly the same a few times before finishing a full circle. Since you love going to the park, can you think of an amusement park ride that spins around and does the exact same thing? 🎡

orchestrator_intent.goal: Re-engage student and introduce rotational symmetry.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: Student is highly distracted, looking away, and off-topic. Engagement is needed to bring focus back to introducing rotational symmetry.

current_section_content: A shape has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same after being rotated (turned) by a fraction of a full turn around its centre point. The fractions we check are 1/4 turn, 1/2 turn, and 3/4 turn. A shape that only looks the same after a full turn does not count as having rotational symmetry. You can track this by placing a dot on the shape and observing where the dot ends up after each turn.
session_summary: The session covered the concept of symmetry and lines of symmetry. The student understood the material well, successfully identifying a vertical line of symmetry on their own body. However, the student struggled significantly with focus and attention, frequently becoming distracted by off-topic subjects like their dog and favorite color. The student's current learning state reflects a solid grasp of basic symmetry, though they require constant redirection from the tutor to stay on task.
